chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Keeping the Time with the Skies

points: The Moon's appearance changes regularly through phases: full Moon (entire illuminated portion faces Earth), waxing period (bright portion increasing, lasts ~15 days), waning period (bright portion decreasing, lasts ~15 days), and new Moon (non-illuminated portion faces Earth, invisible). One complete cycle takes ~29.5 days. These phases occur because the Moon reflects sunlight and only different portions of the sunlit hemisphere face Earth as it orbits; they are NOT caused by Earth's shadow. The Moon appears at different positions in the sky relative to the Sun: farthest during full Moon day, closest during new Moon day. A waxing Moon is easiest to observe at sunset; a waning Moon at sunrise.

The Moon rises approximately 50 minutes later each day because while Earth rotates in 24 hours, the Moon continues moving in its orbit. When the Moon reaches the same position it occupied the previous day, Earth must rotate an additional ~50 minutes. Lunar eclipses and Moon phases are different: lunar eclipses (rare, Earth's shadow on Moon, full Moon day only) cause temporary darkening; Moon phases (regular monthly cycle, relative positioning of celestial bodies, occur nightly) cause changing visible shapes. The precise lunar phase at sunrise varies by region due to different sunrise times across India (earlier in east, later in west).

To keep calendar festivals uniform across India despite regional sunrise variations, the Government of India's Positional Astronomy Center publishes the Rashtriya Panchang annually, calculating celestial object positions for a central location and forecasting festival dates nationwide. Understanding Moon phases helps predict where and when to observe the Moon in the sky on any given day.

Three natural periodic phenomena form time units: day (~24 hours from shortest shadow to shortest shadow, based on Earth's rotation), month (~29.5 days, based on Moon's complete phase cycle), year (~365.25 days, based on Earth's revolution around the Sun and seasonal cycle). Lunar calendars use Moon phases with months of ~29.5 days and years of ~354 days (12 lunar months); they are easy to track but cause seasons to drift relative to months over successive years. Solar calendars (like Gregorian) adjust months to total ~365 days, keeping seasons synchronized to same months yearly; they ignore Moon phases. Luni-solar calendars combine both by using lunar months but adding intercalary months (Adhika Maasa) every 2-3 years (~11-day annual drift accumulates to ~29.5 days) to maintain seasonal synchronization.

The Gregorian calendar accounts for Earth's 365.25-day orbit through leap year rules: add a day every 4 years (divisible by 4), skip leap years every 100 years (1700, 1800, 1900 were not leap years), but add back every 400 years (2000 was a leap year). The Indian National Calendar (adopted 1956) is a solar calendar with months from traditional Indian calendars, beginning on March 22 (day after spring equinox); in leap years it begins March 21. The tropical year (~365.2422 days, Earth's position relative to seasons) guides the Gregorian calendar; the sidereal year (~365.2564 days, Earth's position relative to stars) is ~20 minutes longer and guides some festival calendars.

Many Indian festivals tied to lunar phases (Diwali on new Moon of Kartika, Holi on full Moon of Phalguna, Buddha Purnima on full Moon of Vaisakha, Eid-ul-Fitr after crescent sighting) occur on different Gregorian calendar dates yearly. Luni-solar calendar festivals shift less than a month; purely lunar calendar festivals can shift across different months. Some festivals follow the sidereal solar cycle (Makar Sankranti, Pongal, Bihu, Vaisakhi around January 13-14 or April 13-14) and remain near the same Gregorian date yearly; however, Earth's axis precession causes ~1-day drift every 71 years relative to the winter solstice.

Artificial satellites are human-made objects placed in orbit around Earth (~800 km altitude), completing one orbit in ~100 minutes. Visible as moving points of light in the night sky just before sunrise or after sunset, they serve critical functions: communication (phone, internet, television), navigation (GPS positioning), weather monitoring and forecasting, disaster management and relief coordination, scientific research of stars and space, and Earth observation for mapping, urban planning, agriculture monitoring. The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has launched numerous satellites: Cartosat series for high-resolution Earth imaging, Bhuvan platform displaying terrain and land-use data, AstroSat space telescope, Chandrayaan lunar missions, Aditya L1 solar mission, Mangalyaan Mars orbiter, and student satellites (AzaadiSat, InspireSat-1, Jugnu).

Vikram Sarabhai (1919-1971), known as the Father of the Indian Space Programme, pioneered India's space efforts. The Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre develops rockets and launch vehicles. Space debris—non-functional satellites, spent rocket stages, collision fragments—accumulates in orbit, posing collision hazards to working satellites. High-speed debris impacts can damage or destroy satellites, generating more debris in a cascading effect. Small debris (< 1 cm) burns in atmosphere during reentry; larger pieces can crash on ground. International efforts are developing tracking systems and removal technologies to manage growing space debris hazard.
